ankle_ks_ankle_rocker.progression_to: Weighted ankle rocker, dynamic forward lunge rockers
ankle_ks_ankle_rocker.integration_notes: Excellent daily ankle work. Can be programmed as warm-up staple for anyone doing lower body training.
ankle_ks_ankle_rocker.tempo_dose: 10-15 slow rocks per side, 2 seconds each direction. 2-3 sets per side. 45s rest.

shoulder_eng_press_slow_lower.name: ENG Overhead Press (Slow Eccentric to End-Range Flexion)
shoulder_eng_press_slow_lower.equipment: Landmine, barbell, or DB - 40-70% of normal press weight
shoulder_eng_press_slow_lower.setup: Standing or half-kneeling in pressing setup. Start at top position (arms locked overhead).
shoulder_eng_press_slow_lower.execution: Lower the weight through 4-5 second eccentric phase to full end-range shoulder flexion at the starting position (racked position or chin level). At lowest point, pause 1-2 seconds. Press back up at normal tempo. The eccentric loads the rotator cuff and anterior capsule at end range of shoulder flexion.
indications: Building shoulder flexion capacity under load
Client struggles with control at end range overhead
coach_cues: Slow and controlled on the way down
Pause briefly at the bottom - don't bounce
Keep the scapula engaged throughout
Rib cage down - no lumbar arch to compensate
shoulder_eng_press_slow_lower.progression_from: Landmine press standard tempo, light DB press
shoulder_eng_press_slow_lower.progression_to: Heavy barbell OHP, loaded press variations
shoulder_eng_press_slow_lower.integration_notes: Best done with landmine or DB variants before progressing to barbell. Builds the end-range tissue tolerance needed for heavy overhead work. Essential for clients returning to pressing after shoulder issues.
shoulder_eng_press_slow_lower.tempo_dose: 4-5 seconds down · 1-2 second pause · 1-2 seconds up. 3-4 sets of 5-8 reps. 90s rest.

shoulder_eng_pullup_negative.name: ENG Pull-Up Negative (Slow Descent to Dead Hang)
shoulder_eng_pullup_negative.equipment: Pull-up bar, optional box/step for setup
shoulder_eng_pullup_negative.setup: Jump or step up to the top of the pull-up position (chin over bar).
shoulder_eng_pullup_negative.execution: Lower through a 5-8 second eccentric phase to full dead hang. At bottom, scapula depressed and elevated fully through the available range (passive stretch position). Release or step back up - do not attempt concentric.
indications: Building pull-up strength when full rep unavailable
Lat and scapular capacity at end range
contraindications: Acute shoulder pain at top position
coach_cues: Count the seconds - don't drop
Keep the lats engaged the whole way
Protract the shoulders at the bottom - full stretch
Feel the scapula through the full range
shoulder_eng_pullup_negative.integration_notes: Essential exercise for clients working toward their first pull-up. The eccentric phase builds the strength and tissue tolerance that concentric strength follows from. Program 2-3x/week.
shoulder_eng_pullup_negative.tempo_dose: 5-8 seconds down. 3-5 sets of 3-5 reps. 2 min rest.

shoulder_eqi_bottom_up_kb_hold.name: EQI Bottom-Up KB Overhead Hold
shoulder_eqi_bottom_up_kb_hold.setup: Standing or half-kneeling. KB held overhead with the bell facing up (bottom-up grip), elbow locked.
shoulder_eqi_bottom_up_kb_hold.execution: Hold the bottom-up KB position overhead for 20-45 seconds. The instability forces continuous rotator cuff engagement. Shoulder must actively stabilize the unstable load at full flexion end range.
indications: Rotator cuff active strengthening under load
contraindications: Cannot stabilize bottom-up KB at all
coach_cues: Crush the handle - grip is part of the stability
Ribs down, elbow locked
Micro-adjust constantly - that's the work
If the bell tips, come down immediately
shoulder_eqi_bottom_up_kb_hold.progression_from: Standard overhead KB hold, bottom-up at partial ranges
shoulder_eqi_bottom_up_kb_hold.progression_to: Heavier bottom-up holds, bottom-up press variations
shoulder_eqi_bottom_up_kb_hold.integration_notes: Extreme stability demand. Excellent integration exercise that trains multiple inputs simultaneously at the shoulder. Reserved for advanced clients - program sparingly.
shoulder_eqi_bottom_up_kb_hold.tempo_dose: 20-45 seconds hold. 3 rounds per side. 90s rest.

shoulder_eqi_dead_hang.name: EQI Dead Hang (Full Shoulder Flexion End Range)
shoulder_eqi_dead_hang.setup: Grip pull-up bar, feet off floor, arms fully extended overhead. Passive dead hang position.
shoulder_eqi_dead_hang.execution: Hold the dead hang position for 30-90 seconds. Shoulder in full flexion end range under bodyweight load. Scapula allowed to elevate fully (protraction/elevation). This is loaded end-range passive position.
coach_cues: Relax into the hang - not an active hold
Let the shoulders come up by the ears - that's the stretch
Breathe deeply
Come down if grip fails - don't drop
shoulder_eqi_dead_hang.progression_from: Passive hang with feet touching floor
shoulder_eqi_dead_hang.progression_to: Active hang (scap depression), L-sit progression, weighted hang
shoulder_eqi_dead_hang.integration_notes: Foundational shoulder and grip work. Can be programmed daily for 30-60 seconds. Builds the baseline tissue tolerance that all pulling work sits on top of.

shoulder_ks_wall_slides_loaded.name: Kinetic Stretch Wall Slides (Loaded Band)
shoulder_ks_wall_slides_loaded.equipment: Light band (10-30lb tension) around wrists or overhead anchor
shoulder_ks_wall_slides_loaded.setup: Back against wall, arms in goalpost position with light band tension around wrists or from overhead anchor.
shoulder_ks_wall_slides_loaded.execution: Slide arms up the wall to full flexion (or band overhead position) while maintaining wrist/elbow contact with wall AND maintaining band tension throughout. Active engagement of scapular and rotator cuff throughout the full ROM. Return slowly to goalpost position. The resistance trains the tissue through the full range of motion actively.
coach_cues: Keep wrists on the wall the whole time
Feel the scapula rotate up
Resist the band actively - don't let it snap back
No lumbar arch - ribs down
shoulder_ks_wall_slides_loaded.progression_to: Landmine press progressions, loaded overhead work
shoulder_ks_wall_slides_loaded.integration_notes: Universal staple for upper body warm-ups. Teaches the shoulder to move into end range actively under controlled resistance. Pairs with any press session.
shoulder_ks_wall_slides_loaded.tempo_dose: 2 seconds up · 2 seconds pause at top · 2 seconds down. 3 sets of 8-12 reps. 60s rest.

shoulder_ks_crab_reach.name: Kinetic Stretch Crab Reach (Loaded Thoracic Rotation + Shoulder Extension)
shoulder_ks_crab_reach.setup: Start in table-top position (reverse tabletop) - hands and feet on floor, hips lifted, facing up.
shoulder_ks_crab_reach.execution: From table-top, rotate the body to one side while reaching the opposite arm overhead and across - creating combined thoracic rotation, shoulder extension, and anterior chain loading. Return to table-top, repeat to other side. Continuous smooth motion. Active engagement throughout.
coach_cues: Hips stay high throughout
Reach as far as you can - this is the range
Smooth transitions - don't jerk
Breathe at each end range position
shoulder_ks_crab_reach.progression_to: Animal flow sequences, weighted crab variations
shoulder_ks_crab_reach.integration_notes: Integrates multiple joints and patterns. Excellent finisher or active recovery. Demonstrates full-body full-range training as a single continuous movement.
shoulder_ks_crab_reach.tempo_dose: 2 seconds to reach, 1 second hold at end range, 2 seconds return. 3 sets of 6-8 per side. 60s rest.

shoulder_lir_landmine_press_ramp.name: Loaded Isoramp Landmine Press (Tier-Paused Reps)
shoulder_lir_landmine_press_ramp.equipment: Landmine attachment + barbell, 25-50% of normal press weight
shoulder_lir_landmine_press_ramp.setup: Half-kneeling or standing in landmine press position.
shoulder_lir_landmine_press_ramp.execution: Press the landmine through tier-paused ascent: 3 seconds at quarter range, 3 seconds at half range, 3 seconds at full extension. Active press at each tier - this is concentric isoramping. Lower normally.
indications: Identifying specific weak points in pressing ROM
contraindications: Cannot perform standard landmine press cleanly
Shoulder pain at any pressing range
Cannot maintain half-kneeling position under fatigue
coach_cues: Active pressing at each pause - not a rest
Find the weak tier - that's the target
Full ROM - don't short-change the finish
Control breathing through the pauses
shoulder_lir_landmine_press_ramp.progression_to: Loaded isoramp DB press, loaded isoramp barbell press
shoulder_lir_landmine_press_ramp.integration_notes: Diagnostic + training exercise. Reveals exactly where a client's press breaks down. Advanced clients only - cognitively demanding and metabolically taxing.
shoulder_lir_landmine_press_ramp.tempo_dose: 3 second pauses at 3 tiers during press. 3 sets of 3-5 reps per side. 2 min rest.

scap_eng_row_slow_return.name: ENG Row (Slow Eccentric with Full Protraction)
scap_eng_row_slow_return.equipment: Cable, bands, or DB - moderate load
scap_eng_row_slow_return.setup: Standing or seated in rowing position with load.
scap_eng_row_slow_return.execution: Pull through standard concentric (1-2 seconds). Release through 4-5 second eccentric to full scapular protraction at end range. Feel the scapulae reach apart fully. Pause 1-2 seconds in the fully protracted position before next rep.
contraindications: Acute shoulder impingement at end range
Cannot reach full protraction without compensatory forward lean
coach_cues: Let the shoulder blade WRAP around the ribs at the end
Feel the stretch between the shoulder blades
Slow return - don't collapse back
Chest stays up - don't hunch
scap_eng_row_slow_return.progression_to: Heavier ENG rows, single-arm ENG rows
scap_eng_row_slow_return.integration_notes: Addresses the typical upper back weakness that pervades adult clients. The slow eccentric trains the retractors to work through full range, not just the middle.
scap_eng_row_slow_return.tempo_dose: 1-2 seconds pull · 4-5 seconds return · 1-2 second pause stretched. 3-4 sets of 8-12 reps. 60-90s rest.

elbow_eng_bicep_curl_slow.name: ENG Bicep Curl (Slow Eccentric to Full Extension)
elbow_eng_bicep_curl_slow.setup: Standing or seated, DBs at sides (arms extended).
elbow_eng_bicep_curl_slow.execution: Curl concentric at 1-2 seconds to full flexion. Lower through 4-5 second eccentric to full elbow extension with slight bicep/tendon stretch feel. Pause 1 second at bottom - do NOT bounce out. Tension maintained throughout.
indications: Building elbow extension tolerance under load
contraindications: Hyperextended elbows (limit extension to just short)
coach_cues: Full extension at the bottom - don't shortcut
Slow lower - elbow, wrist, shoulder controlled
Squeeze at the top
Pause at the bottom briefly
elbow_eng_bicep_curl_slow.progression_to: Heavier ENG curls, incline dumbbell curl for greater bicep stretch
elbow_eng_bicep_curl_slow.integration_notes: Often overlooked - the elbow needs full-range work too. The eccentric emphasis protects the bicep tendon while building strength through full ROM.
elbow_eng_bicep_curl_slow.tempo_dose: 1-2 seconds up · 4-5 seconds down · 1 second pause stretched. 3 sets of 10-15 reps. 60s rest.

wrist_eqi_hanging_finger_stretch.name: EQI Hanging Wrist Extension (Finger-Only Hang Progression)
wrist_eqi_hanging_finger_stretch.setup: Grip pull-up bar with fingers only (no palm contact). Feet bearing some weight (regression) or hanging fully (progression).
wrist_eqi_hanging_finger_stretch.execution: Hold the hanging position with fingers only. Wrist flexors are loaded in deep extension. Duration based on tolerance - start 5-10 seconds, build to 30.
contraindications: Cannot perform standard dead hang for 30s
coach_cues: Let the wrists stretch - that's the point
Start with feet touching - progress gradually
Come down before grip fails
Protect the fingers - don't overload too fast
wrist_eqi_hanging_finger_stretch.progression_from: Standard dead hang, forearm wall holds
wrist_eqi_hanging_finger_stretch.progression_to: Full hanging finger holds, single-arm finger hangs
wrist_eqi_hanging_finger_stretch.integration_notes: Highly niche. Climbers, gymnasts, elite grip athletes. Not for general population. Extreme tissue demand.

tspine_ks_rotation_loaded.name: Kinetic Stretch T-Spine Rotation (Cable)
tspine_ks_rotation_loaded.equipment: Cable machine or band, moderate load
tspine_ks_rotation_loaded.setup: Standing in staggered stance OR half-kneeling perpendicular to cable. Handle held at chest height.
tspine_ks_rotation_loaded.execution: Rotate the torso through full range - pulling the cable across body (from opposite side). Active engagement through full rotation. At end range, pause 1-2 seconds feeling the opposite-side obliques lengthen. Return slowly. The load challenges rotation through the full ROM.
contraindications: Cannot achieve clean T-spine rotation without lumbar compensation
coach_cues: Rotate from the mid-back, not the low back
Feel the stretch at end range
Control the return - don't let it snap back
Keep the hips stable
tspine_ks_rotation_loaded.progression_to: Heavier rotation patterns, Pallof press variations, med ball throws
tspine_ks_rotation_loaded.integration_notes: Central exercise for rotational sport prep. Also addresses the universal T-spine rotation deficit in desk-bound adults. Pairs with T-spine iso ramping.
tspine_ks_rotation_loaded.tempo_dose: 2 seconds to rotate · 1-2 seconds pause at end range · 2 seconds return. 3 sets of 6-10 per side. 60s rest.

tspine_eqi_extension_overhead_hold.name: EQI T-Spine Extension Overhead Hold (Loaded)
tspine_eqi_extension_overhead_hold.setup: Standing or tall kneeling with DB held overhead, arms fully extended.
tspine_eqi_extension_overhead_hold.execution: Hold the overhead position for 30-60 seconds with active T-spine extension - open the chest, upper back extends, ribs stay down (no lumbar compensation). The light load creates a loaded end-range extension hold.
indications: T-spine extension restriction (universal in desk workers)
contraindications: Cannot achieve overhead position without lumbar arch
coach_cues: Ribs DOWN - don't rib-flare to cheat extension
The chest opens - the back bone extends
Breathe evenly through it
Arms stay fully locked
tspine_eqi_extension_overhead_hold.progression_to: Heavier loaded overhead holds, single-arm versions
tspine_eqi_extension_overhead_hold.integration_notes: Essential T-spine work. Addresses one of the most common restrictions in adult clients. The loaded overhead position is both a mobility challenge and a strength requirement.

lumbar_eng_jefferson_curl.name: ENG Jefferson Curl (Slow Eccentric Spinal Flexion)
lumbar_eng_jefferson_curl.equipment: Light DB or KB 5-15lb ONLY (NOT heavy)
lumbar_eng_jefferson_curl.setup: Standing on elevated surface (step/bench) with weight held in front.
lumbar_eng_jefferson_curl.execution: With knees locked straight, roll down vertebra-by-vertebra through 8-10 second eccentric phase. Feel each segment of the spine flex under the load. At the bottom (deepest spinal flexion), pause briefly. Roll back up slowly, reversing the movement segment-by-segment.
indications: Addressing chronic flexion-intolerance through progressive exposure
ONLY for clients with no acute back pain
contraindications: General population clients - use with extreme caution
coach_cues: Roll down ONE vertebra at a time
Feel each segment - don't bunch them up
Use LIGHT weight only
If any pain - STOP immediately
lumbar_eng_jefferson_curl.progression_to: Heavier Jefferson curls (rare - most clients stop at light loads)
lumbar_eng_jefferson_curl.integration_notes: VERY selective use. Most IMS clients will never be programmed this. Reserved for advanced movement-quality clients (gymnasts, dancers) with no back history. Included for completeness, not general programming.
lumbar_eng_jefferson_curl.tempo_dose: 8-10 seconds down · brief pause · 8-10 seconds up. 2-3 sets of 3-5 reps. 2-3 min rest.

cervical_eqi_chin_tuck_hold.name: EQI Loaded Chin Tuck Hold
cervical_eqi_chin_tuck_hold.setup: Supine, back of head on floor or block.
cervical_eqi_chin_tuck_hold.execution: Actively tuck chin and maintain the deep cervical flexor engagement for 30-60 seconds. Active hold, not passive. If progressing, light manual resistance applied to forehead by coach or self (push back against resistance).
coach_cues: Double chin feel - that's the engagement
Back of head pressed lightly to floor
Breathe steady
No straining - gentle sustained effort
cervical_eqi_chin_tuck_hold.progression_to: Against light manual resistance, integrated with upper body training
cervical_eqi_chin_tuck_hold.integration_notes: Cervical work is always conservative at IMS. Cap duration and intensity. Best programmed alongside T-spine extension work to address full postural chain.

hip_lir_rdl_ramp.name: Loaded Isoramp RDL (Tier-Paused Eccentric)
hip_lir_rdl_ramp.equipment: KB/DB or barbell, 40-60% of training max
hip_lir_rdl_ramp.execution: Descend through tier-paused eccentric: pause 3 seconds at quarter depth, 3 seconds at mid-hinge, 3 seconds at full end-range hinge. At each pause, actively engage hamstrings. Return normally. Tier ladder reveals the range where capacity is weakest.
contraindications: Low back pain at any hinge depth
coach_cues: Active engagement at each tier - hamstrings on
Find the weak tier - that's the target
Neutral spine throughout
Breathe between tiers
hip_lir_rdl_ramp.integration_notes: Advanced diagnostic. Shows exactly which range of the hinge needs work.
hip_lir_rdl_ramp.tempo_dose: 3s pauses at 3 tiers during descent. 3 sets of 4-5 reps. 2 min rest.

hip_ks_lateral_lunge_continuous.name: Kinetic Stretch Continuous Lateral Lunge
hip_ks_lateral_lunge_continuous.execution: Shift into lateral lunge on one side (straight opposite leg stretches adductors). Continuously flow to opposite side - the working leg and stretched leg alternate without returning to center. Active engagement both sides throughout. Full range each rep.
contraindications: Cannot hold end-range lateral lunge for 5s
coach_cues: Don't return to center - flow through
Active adductor engagement on the stretched side
Full depth each rep
Smooth continuous movement
hip_ks_lateral_lunge_continuous.progression_to: Heavier loaded lateral patterns, dynamic lateral bounds
hip_ks_lateral_lunge_continuous.integration_notes: Excellent conditioning-adjacent full-range work. Great for athletic prep blocks.
hip_ks_lateral_lunge_continuous.tempo_dose: 2 seconds each side, continuous flow. 3 sets of 8-12 total reps (4-6 per side). 60s rest.

hip_eqi_single_leg_rdl_hold.name: EQI Single-Leg RDL End Range Hold
hip_eqi_single_leg_rdl_hold.setup: Standing on one leg, weight in opposite hand (contralateral loading).
hip_eqi_single_leg_rdl_hold.execution: Hinge into single-leg RDL position at end range. Hold the deepest position with rear leg parallel to floor, weight approaching shin height, neutral spine. Hold 20-45 seconds. Active engagement throughout - not a collapsed position.
contraindications: Cannot hold single-leg balance for 20s without weight
coach_cues: Hips square - don't let the stance hip open up
Rear leg kicks back and up
Weight stays close to the support leg
Find the end range and hold
hip_eqi_single_leg_rdl_hold.integration_notes: Demanding integration exercise. Reveals asymmetries immediately.
hip_eqi_single_leg_rdl_hold.tempo_dose: 20-45 seconds hold. 3 rounds per side. 90-120s rest.

hip_eng_reverse_lunge_slow.name: ENG Reverse Lunge (Slow Descent)
hip_eng_reverse_lunge_slow.execution: Step one foot back into reverse lunge through 4-second controlled eccentric phase. Rear knee just above floor. Pause 1-2 seconds at bottom. Drive back up to standing. Alternate sides.
indications: Hip extension capacity under dynamic load
coach_cues: Slow step back - don't rush
Upright torso
Drop STRAIGHT down - not forward
Drive through the front foot to stand
hip_eng_reverse_lunge_slow.integration_notes: Bridge exercise between static split squat and dynamic lunging patterns.
hip_eng_reverse_lunge_slow.tempo_dose: 4s descent · 1-2s pause · 1-2s ascent. 3 sets of 6-8 per side. 90s rest.

knee_ks_heel_elevated_goblet.name: Kinetic Stretch Heel-Elevated Goblet Squat
knee_ks_heel_elevated_goblet.equipment: KB/DB goblet, 20-40% BW. Heels elevated on plates 1-2 inches.
knee_ks_heel_elevated_goblet.setup: Feet on plates/wedge, goblet KB at chest.
knee_ks_heel_elevated_goblet.execution: Descend into deep squat with quad emphasis (heel elevation puts more demand on knee flexion). Full depth, upright torso. Active engagement throughout range. Return at normal tempo. Not an ENG (no slow eccentric emphasis) - emphasis is on full range with continuous load.
indications: Client with limited ankle DF (heel elevation compensates)
contraindications: Cannot tolerate deep squat with heel elevation
coach_cues: Heels down on the plates
Upright torso - quads take the work
Full depth each rep
Drive through the whole foot
knee_ks_heel_elevated_goblet.integration_notes: Essential for clients with ankle restriction who still need to train knee flexion capacity.
knee_ks_heel_elevated_goblet.tempo_dose: 2 seconds down · 1 second pause · 1-2 seconds up. 3-4 sets of 8-10 reps. 90s rest.

shoulder_eng_chest_supported_row.name: ENG Chest-Supported Row (Slow Release to Full Stretch)
shoulder_eng_chest_supported_row.equipment: Chest-supported row bench or incline bench + DBs
shoulder_eng_chest_supported_row.setup: Prone on incline bench, DBs hanging straight down.
shoulder_eng_chest_supported_row.execution: Pull through concentric (1-2 seconds) to full scapular retraction at chest level. Release through 4-5 second eccentric to full scapular protraction + shoulder flexion end range. Pause 1 second at deep stretch. Repeat.
indications: Addressing rotation or cheat at end range
contraindications: Cannot maintain chest contact under fatigue
coach_cues: Chest stays on the bench the whole time
Slow release - feel the shoulder blade spread
Pause at the bottom
Pull with elbow toward hip
shoulder_eng_chest_supported_row.progression_to: Single-arm ENG rows, cable ENG rows
shoulder_eng_chest_supported_row.integration_notes: Eliminates cheating via body english. The bench forces clean form through full range.
shoulder_eng_chest_supported_row.tempo_dose: 1-2s pull · 4-5s release · 1s pause. 3-4 sets of 8-12 reps. 60s rest.

shoulder_ks_turkish_getup_partial.name: Kinetic Stretch Turkish Get-Up (Partial Flow)
shoulder_ks_turkish_getup_partial.setup: Supine with KB pressed overhead on one side.
shoulder_ks_turkish_getup_partial.execution: Move through the first 3-4 stations of Turkish Get-Up with full engagement: supine to elbow → elbow to hand → bridge → leg sweep to kneeling. At each station, pause and actively stabilize. Focus is on loaded shoulder stabilization through changing trunk positions. Reverse back to supine.
indications: Shoulder stabilization through full body patterns
coach_cues: Eyes on the bell the whole time
Active stabilization at each station
Slow and controlled - this is precision work
Reverse the pattern back down
shoulder_ks_turkish_getup_partial.integration_notes: Premier integration exercise. Trains shoulder under multiple demands simultaneously.
shoulder_ks_turkish_getup_partial.tempo_dose: Slow controlled movement through stations. 2-3 reps per side. 2-3 min rest.

tspine_ks_windmill.name: Kinetic Stretch KB Windmill
tspine_ks_windmill.setup: Standing with KB pressed overhead, feet at 45-degree offset stance, KB side hip at slight posterior.
tspine_ks_windmill.execution: Keeping the KB locked overhead and eyes on it, slowly hinge laterally - sliding the opposite hand down the inside of the straight leg toward the ankle. At end range, pause 2-3 seconds. Reverse slowly to standing. The T-spine rotates and laterally flexes under the overhead load.
indications: Combined rotation + lateral flexion + shoulder stability
contraindications: Acute shoulder or low back pain
coach_cues: Eyes on the bell throughout
Hinge at the hip - don't just bend sideways
Keep the front leg straight
Slow and controlled - this is precision
tspine_ks_windmill.integration_notes: Sophisticated full-body integration exercise. Rare in general programming but extremely useful for advanced clients.
tspine_ks_windmill.tempo_dose: 3 seconds descent · 2-3 second pause · 3 seconds return. 3 sets of 3-5 per side. 90-120s rest.

ankle_lir_calf_raise_ramp.name: Loaded Isoramp Calf Raise (Tier-Paused)
ankle_lir_calf_raise_ramp.setup: Single leg on step edge, ball of foot on edge, load held or worn.
ankle_lir_calf_raise_ramp.execution: Rise through tier-paused concentric: pause 3 seconds at quarter range, 3 seconds at mid range, 3 seconds at full plantar flexion. Active push through each tier. Lower normally.
indications: Achilles tendon loading through full ROM
contraindications: Cannot perform standard calf raise cleanly
coach_cues: Active push at each tier
Full range each rep
Don't rush the pauses
Stable balance throughout
ankle_lir_calf_raise_ramp.integration_notes: Advanced tendon-health work for runners and jumping athletes.
ankle_lir_calf_raise_ramp.tempo_dose: 3s pauses at 3 tiers during rise. 3 sets of 4-6 per side. 90s rest.

foot_ks_single_leg_balance_dynamic.name: Kinetic Stretch Dynamic Single-Leg Balance (3D Reach)
foot_ks_single_leg_balance_dynamic.equipment: Bodyweight, optional 5-10lb medicine ball or DB
foot_ks_single_leg_balance_dynamic.execution: While balancing on one leg, reach the free leg to 4 directions (forward, lateral, diagonal, posterior) and the opposite arm to 3-4 directions. Active foot engagement throughout - short foot feel, toes active. Continuous slow movement without hopping or losing balance.
indications: Fall risk reduction in older adults
contraindications: Cannot stand on one leg for 20 seconds
coach_cues: Active foot - short foot, toes engaged
Reach slowly - don't hop
Keep hips level
Eyes ahead, chin up
foot_ks_single_leg_balance_dynamic.integration_notes: High-value for the 50+ IMS client. Fall prevention + performance training.
foot_ks_single_leg_balance_dynamic.tempo_dose: 8-12 reaches per side. 2-3 rounds per side. 60s rest.

coach_cues: Open the hip like a gate

coaching_cues: Keep legs as straight as you can
coach_cues: Walk hands out to plank

Punch opposite arm

wu_high_knees_linear_lateral.name: High Knees (linear and lateral)

Quick ground contact

wu_cariocas.name: Cariocas

Rhythmic

wu_shuffles.name: Shuffles

Push off the outside foot

Reach the arm through

corr_straight_leg_raises.name: Straight Leg Raises

corr_straight_leg_lowers.name: Straight Leg Lowers

corr_on_horse_off_horse.name: On Horse Off Horse
coaching_cues: Swing leg over an imaginary horse

corr_cats_and_cows.name: Cat and Cow
coaching_cues: Move one segment at a time
coach_cues: Move one segment at a time
Breathe with the movement
Full flexion, full extension

corr_scap_retraction_protraction.name: All-Fours Scapular Retraction/Protraction

Retract - pull blades together

corr_bird_dog.name: Bird Dog

corr_reach_through_rotate.name: Reach Through and Rotate (Thread the Needle)

Hip stays stacked

corr_hockey_rockers.name: Hockey Rockers

Explore the rotation
Knees wide

corr_sl_hockey_rockers.name: Single Leg Hockey Rockers

Rock into the front hip

corr_hip_flexor_rockers.name: Hip Flexor Rockers

Glute on

coach_cues: Whatever the start, explode

power_reactive_multi_vert.name: Reactive Vertical Jumps (Multiple)

Feet flat, toes slightly out
Chest tall

coach_cues: Brace before unrack

coach_cues: Slight knee bend, hips drive back

coach_cues: Bar stays close to the body

Back leg is an extension of the spine

Press straight up

pushh_db_bench.name: Dumbbell Bench Press (Single or Double Arm)

Drive the floor with your feet

Bar to lower chest

Press through the chest

pushv_seated_ohp.name: Seated Overhead Press (Single or Double Arm)

Back glute on, ribs down

playbook_hinge.name: Hinge — Playbook
playbook_hinge.execution:
The hinge is the foundation of lower-body strength, posterior chain health, and real-world lifting capacity. At IMS we treat it as a skill first and a loaded pattern second. Most adults over 35 have lost the ability to hinge cleanly - they squat when they think they're hinging, their lumbar does the work their hips should be doing, and they load a broken pattern for years.

The hinge is prioritized in every lower-body block. It is the first lift we teach because a client who owns the hinge can protect their back for the rest of their life. We favor intelligent progression - KB and landmine options before barbell - because the barbell isn't magic and most adults don't need a conventional deadlift to get strong. We favor unilateral work at or above bilateral once the pattern is owned, because asymmetry is the source of most adult hip and back issues.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: Hips back, not down. If the client is thinking 'sit', they're squatting. If they're thinking 'push hips to the wall behind me', they're hinging.
WATCH FOR: Lumbar flexion at the bottom. Look at the low back from the side. If the curve reverses, the set stops. Full stop.
WATCH FOR: Knees caving forward instead of shins staying vertical. The hinge is a HIP exercise - knees bend because they have to, not because they want to.
WATCH FOR: Shoulder rounding. The upper back collapses before the low back gives up. Cue 'proud chest' or 'long spine' before every rep.
WATCH FOR: Grip failure dictating set length. If the bar is slipping out of the hands, you're training grip, not hinge. Rotate which one is the limiter.
WATCH FOR: Breath held through the full rep. Adults lock up their breath under load. Cue exhale on the concentric, inhale at the top. Breathing is not optional.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: If something isn't working, go back to the dowel. Reset the pattern, then rebuild. Never add load to a broken pattern.
PAIR WITH: PAILs/RAILs at flagged hip capsular targets. Hip flexion capacity is the ceiling on deadlift depth. Train the capacity, the strength follows.

PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Hinge volume lives in strength blocks, not conditioning blocks. Kettlebell swings are hinge-ballistic and belong in power or conditioning - they are NOT a replacement for loaded hinge work.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Unilateral first in the block once the pattern is owned. A set of SL RDL before a set of BB RDL exposes asymmetries before heavy loading hides them.

playbook_squat.name: Squat — Playbook
playbook_squat.execution:
The squat at IMS is treated as a joint capacity demonstration, not a maximum loading event. We care about the quality of the bottom position more than the plate count. A clean bodyweight squat at 110 degrees of hip flexion, 30 degrees of ankle dorsiflexion, and a neutral spine is more valuable than a heavy back squat performed with a butt wink and a folded thoracic spine.

We progress the squat conservatively for the IMS demographic. Goblet squat is home base for 60% of our clients - it's enough loading to drive strength adaptations, and it builds the front-loaded core demand that transfers to every other pattern. We teach the landmine goblet and front squat as intermediate progressions before most clients ever see a barbell back squat. Unilateral squatting (split squat, RFESS, eventually SL squat) gets equal time with bilateral - asymmetry work is prioritized, not tacked on.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Sit between the hips, not onto them.' Most squat compensations come from the client collapsing onto one side or folding forward.
WATCH FOR: Butt wink at the bottom. Lumbar flexion under load is the first sign the depth is past the client's hip capacity. Shorten ROM or program hip flexion capacity work.
WATCH FOR: Knees caving inward (valgus collapse). Usually glute med weakness or hip IR restriction. Add banded squats for the weakness; add hip CARs/PAILs for the restriction.
WATCH FOR: Forward dump of the torso. Ankle DF, hip flexion, or T-spine extension is the limiter. Check which one and address.
WATCH FOR: Heel rise at the bottom. Ankle DF is insufficient or client is reaching for depth they don't have. Elevate heels or reduce ROM.
WATCH FOR: Bar position slipping on back squat. If the bar is migrating, the setup is wrong or thoracic extension is lacking. Reset between every set.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: If something isn't working, go to goblet squat. Front-loaded, simple, forgiving. Rebuild from there.
PAIR WITH: Hip CARs (all levels), ankle CARs, and hip flexion PAILs/RAILs for clients with depth restriction. Ankle DF PAILs/RAILs for heel-rise issues.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Unilateral volume >= bilateral volume for most IMS clients. Adult asymmetries are the rule, not the exception.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Don't chase heavy back squats if front squats are training the pattern. The back squat is not the king of lifts at IMS - the correctly-loaded pattern is.

playbook_push_horizontal.name: Push Horizontal — Playbook
playbook_push_horizontal.execution:
Horizontal pressing is trained at IMS with the understanding that most adults walk in with some degree of anterior shoulder capsule tightness, posterior capsule looseness, or scapular protraction dysfunction. We respect this. We favor variations that allow natural scapular motion - dumbbell over barbell, floor press before bench, neutral grip before pronated. We program pushing volume at roughly half the volume of pulling volume. Always. Not sometimes. Always.

The horizontal press is a main lift when programmed - not an afterthought, not a finisher. When it's in the block, it earns its place. When it's not, we're not defaulting to bench press because we feel we should. Sometimes the right answer for a client with irritable shoulders is to stop horizontal pressing for 4-8 weeks and rebuild through pulls and position-specific mobility work.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Elbows 45, not 90.' This single cue prevents 80% of the shoulder issues in adult pressing.
WATCH FOR: Scapular hiking on the press. If the shoulder blade rides up toward the ear, traps are dominating and scap control is failing. Regress the load.
WATCH FOR: Lower back arching under load. Anti-extension weakness or lazy bracing. Ribs over pelvis, glutes on, every rep.
WATCH FOR: Forward head posture under load. Usually indicates the load is too heavy or the client is reaching through the neck instead of pressing through the chest.
WATCH FOR: Wrist collapse under load. Wrist stays in line with the forearm - no bent-back bar carrying.
WATCH FOR: Elbow flare to 90 degrees under fatigue. First sign set should end.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to floor press or eccentric pushup. Reset scap control and ROM, rebuild.
PAIR WITH: Shoulder CARs and PAILs/RAILs on posterior capsule (IR in flexion) for most pressing clients. Their bench will improve because their joint will open.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Press volume is half of pull volume. Track it. Count sets. Don't cheat this rule.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Don't press through shoulder pain to preserve a program. The program serves the client, not vice versa.

playbook_push_vertical.name: Push Vertical — Playbook
playbook_push_vertical.execution:
Vertical pressing is a joint-capacity-limited pattern more than any other. You cannot press overhead cleanly without shoulder flexion, thoracic extension, and scapular upward rotation all working together. A huge portion of adults 35-65 are missing one or more of these by the time they walk into IMS. We respect that. We do not force clients into straight overhead pressing until they've earned the capacity.

The landmine press changes the equation. It delivers 80% of the benefits of true overhead pressing - anti-extension demand, vertical chain integration, shoulder capacity under load - with 70% of the joint demand. For a lot of clients, the standing landmine press IS their vertical press, forever. That's not a regression. That's intelligent programming for the population we serve.

For clients who can press overhead cleanly, we build volume in the pattern with respect - seated progressions before standing, half-kneeling before full-standing, DB before BB. The BB overhead press is for advanced clients who have demonstrated the capacity. It is not a default.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Ribs down, ribs down, ribs down.' Every rep. Anti-extension is half of every overhead press.
WATCH FOR: Lower back arching to finish the rep. The bar finishes overhead by the client leaning back, not pressing up. Stop the set.
WATCH FOR: Elbows flaring wide at lockout. Scap control failing or shoulder ER insufficient. Regress.
WATCH FOR: Neck jutting forward at lockout. Cervical strategy, not shoulder strategy. Reset posture between reps.
WATCH FOR: Chin tucked hard throughout. Over-bracing. Neutral neck, eyes forward.
WATCH FOR: Wrist collapsed back under the DB. Wrist stacks over the elbow, not behind it.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to standing landmine, then half-kneeling landmine. Rebuild the anti-extension brace at lower shoulder demand.
PAIR WITH: Shoulder CARs (all levels), T-spine CARs, shoulder flexion PAILs/RAILs, and T-spine extension PAILs/RAILs. Vertical pressing is capacity-gated.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Landmine pressing is a real vertical press, not a warm-up. Program it in strength blocks with intent.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: If a client has to muscle through anti-extension to press, they're not ready to press. Build the core, then load the shoulder.

playbook_pull_horizontal.name: Pull Horizontal — Playbook
playbook_pull_horizontal.execution:
Horizontal pulling is the most programmed category at IMS after CARs. Every upper body block includes horizontal pulling at 2:1 volume against horizontal pressing. This is the counter to modern life - desk posture, phone-looking, driving - all of which load the anterior chain and de-load the posterior upper body. We program pulls to reverse that pattern.

We favor variations that isolate the pattern from spine demand when clients have back issues (chest-supported rows, TRX rows) and we integrate spine demand deliberately when clients are ready (standing landmine T-bar, single-arm DB row). The cable pull is the everyday workhorse - the dual adjustable pulleys make this the easiest-to-program horizontal pull at IMS. Every chest-supported, cable-resisted, single-arm, bilateral, neutral-grip, pronated-grip variant is available without leaving the DAP setup.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Pull the elbow to the hip.' Or 'pull the elbow past the ribs.' This keeps the elbow driving and the biceps quiet.
WATCH FOR: Rising shoulders (shrug compensation). Traps are dominating. Cue 'shoulders away from ears' before every set.
WATCH FOR: Spine extension or flexion at the top of the row. Spine stays stacked - only the arm moves.
WATCH FOR: Biceps-only pull. If the elbows are bending before the shoulder blades retract, the pattern is wrong.
WATCH FOR: Hip hinge collapsing into lumbar flexion during the pull. If the spine curves, the set stops.
WATCH FOR: Rotation during single-arm standing rows. Hips and ribs stay square - the resistance wants to rotate them, the core says no.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to chest-supported variations. Remove spine demand, isolate the pull.
PAIR WITH: Scapular CARs (all levels), shoulder CARs, T-spine CARs. Scapular control is the gatekeeper to all pulling adaptation.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: 2:1 pull:push volume. Track weekly. If pull volume dips below 2x push, rebalance.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Mix bilateral and unilateral every block. Unilateral exposes asymmetry, bilateral builds absolute strength.

playbook_pull_vertical.name: Pull Vertical — Playbook
playbook_pull_vertical.execution:
Vertical pulling is programmed at IMS with an honest acknowledgment: most adult clients can't do a strict pull-up when they start, and many never will. That's not a failure - it's reality. We build vertical pulling capacity through a deliberate ladder, starting with cable pulldowns and ending at weighted chin-ups for clients who earn that level.

The progression is slow and respected. We don't give clients a resistance band tied to a pull-up bar and call it progress - band-assisted pull-ups create a weird force curve that doesn't transfer well. We use cable variations with programmable load, negative pull-ups for eccentric strength, and we let clients live at lat pulldown variations for years if that's what serves them.

Vertical pulling serves shoulder health specifically - it trains the inferior scapular stabilizers and loads the lats in a way horizontal pulling doesn't. Every upper body block at IMS includes at least one vertical pulling movement in addition to horizontal pulling volume.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Pull the elbows down and back.' Drives scap depression and retraction simultaneously.
WATCH FOR: Shoulders hiking toward ears on pulldowns. Scap depression failing. Reset between reps.
WATCH FOR: Kipping on pull-ups. No hip drive, no body swing. Dead start, dead stop.
WATCH FOR: Incomplete ROM - not reaching full hang at bottom, not clearing chin at top. Partial reps don't count.
WATCH FOR: Head jutting forward at top to 'get the chin over.' Cervical compensation - the head is not the goal, the scap retraction is.
WATCH FOR: Elbow flare past 90 degrees at the bottom. Shoulder internal rotation instead of scap depression.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to lat pulldown or cable X pulldown. Rebuild scap control and load the lats under control.
PAIR WITH: Scap CARs (upward rotation and depression emphasis), shoulder CARs, T-spine extension work. Vertical pulling is scap-gated.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: One vertical pull variation per upper body block, minimum. Not every block needs pull-ups - pulldowns are a real pull.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Don't chase pull-ups at the cost of shoulder health. If the client isn't ready, they're not ready.

playbook_rotational_core.name: Rotational Core — Playbook
playbook_rotational_core.execution:
The IMS core philosophy follows the McGill / Cressey / Boyle lineage: the trunk is trained to resist motion more often than produce it, and when we do produce rotation, it comes through the hips and T-spine - not the lumbar. Sit-ups and Russian twists don't appear at IMS. They load the spine in flexion and rotation simultaneously - the exact combination that drives disc pathology.

The core category splits into three: anti-rotation (most common, most programmed), rotational production (for clients ready to train rotation properly), and anti-rotation with load progression (Pallof to chops/lifts). We use half-kneeling and tall-kneeling positions heavily because they lock out the lower body and force the trunk to do the work - a cable chop in standing is only a core exercise if the hips don't cheat.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Hips lock, trunk works.' Or the reverse for rotational production: 'Hips and shoulders rotate together.'
WATCH FOR: Lumbar rotation during rotational work. Look at the belt line - if the belt rotates ahead of the ribs, the low back is doing the rotating.
WATCH FOR: Shoulder elevation during Pallof press or chops. Shrugging = trunk failure. Cue 'shoulders away from ears.'
WATCH FOR: Breath holding. Anti-rotation under breath-hold is just a brace, not a core exercise. Breathe through the load.
WATCH FOR: Hips dumping forward during standing anti-rotation. Cue 'glutes on, ribs over pelvis.'
WATCH FOR: Moving arms without moving trunk. A Pallof press where the arms extend but the trunk twists is not a Pallof press - it's arm extension.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to tall kneeling. Remove lower body complexity. Force the trunk to work.
PAIR WITH: T-spine CARs and rotation PAILs/RAILs. Rotational capacity at the T-spine is the ceiling on any rotational exercise.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Anti-rotation volume > rotational production volume for most IMS clients.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Core work integrated into warm-ups (dead bug, bird dog) AND programmed as separate block (chops, lifts, Pallof). Not either-or.

playbook_carries.name: Carries — Playbook
playbook_carries.execution:
Loaded carries are one of the most under-programmed, highest-ROI patterns in adult training. A single loaded carry trains grip, posture, anti-lateral flexion, anti-extension, gait mechanics, and breath control simultaneously. For the IMS demographic, the carry is often more valuable than any bilateral grinding lift.

We program carries in every strength block and every conditioning block at IMS. They're the connective tissue between strength and metabolic work. Carries also serve as programming when other patterns are contraindicated - a client with an acute knee can still farmer carry. A client with a shoulder flare can still do ipsilateral carries on the healthy side. Carries are low-risk, high-yield, and the progression is infinite (load, distance, position, unilateral vs bilateral).
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Walk with purpose. Don't shuffle.' Steady gait, tall posture, controlled breath.
WATCH FOR: Forward head posture under load. Client looking at the floor. Cue 'eyes up, crown of head tall.'
WATCH FOR: Shoulder shrug under grip demand. Traps taking over. Cue 'shoulders down, grip crushed.'
WATCH FOR: Lateral lean during suitcase carry. If the ribcage shifts to the loaded side, core is failing. Stop, reset, reduce load.
WATCH FOR: Hip hike under load. Opposite hip rising to clear the foot. Glute med weakness - cue 'level hips' or reduce load.
WATCH FOR: Breath-holding. Carries take minutes sometimes. Breath has to keep flowing.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to unloaded walk or lighter farmer carry. Rebuild posture, add load.
PAIR WITH: Grip work (specifically programmed as a capacity), T-spine CARs, scap CARs. Wrist and shoulder CARs before overhead carries.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Carries can be strength (heavy, short distance), conditioning (moderate, long distance), or skill (bottoms-up, asymmetric). All three have a place.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Carries are low-risk programming for clients in transition - post-injury, post-travel, irritable joints. When in doubt, carry something.

playbook_anti_extension.name: Anti Extension — Playbook
playbook_anti_extension.execution:
Anti-extension training at IMS is the direct counter to the dominant lumbar extension pattern most adults carry - anterior pelvic tilt, flared ribs, extended low back. We train anti-extension as both a corrective (fixing posture) and a performance foundation (bracing for every loaded lift). Every client at IMS does anti-extension work. No exceptions.

We progress anti-extension deliberately. Dead bug is the entry and the gate - a client who can't do dead bug cleanly has no business doing rollouts or weighted planks. The common mistake is letting clients skip the basic patterns because they 'feel too easy' - in reality, dead bug done with focus is harder than it looks, and most adults never actually master it.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Ribs over pelvis, glutes on.' Every anti-extension exercise starts with these two things.
WATCH FOR: Low back lifting off the floor in dead bug. The primary failure mode. Reset, reduce ROM.
WATCH FOR: Hip sag in plank. Glutes turning off. Cue 'tuck the pelvis, squeeze the glutes.'
WATCH FOR: Breath-holding during plank. Brace is supposed to hold under breathing, not hide when breath stops.
WATCH FOR: Neck flexion into the chest in hollow hold. Cervical compensation. Neutral neck.
WATCH FOR: Pelvis dropping at the extended position of rollout. The spine extends under load - stop the set immediately.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to dead bug. Reset the pattern. Rebuild.
PAIR WITH: Glute activation work (bridge variations) and hip flexor mobility. Anti-extension capacity is often limited by hip flexor tightness driving pelvic tilt.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Program anti-extension work in every session, in warm-ups or in blocks. Never a workout without it.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Quality > quantity. A 30-second active plank is more valuable than a 2-minute compensated one.

playbook_anti_lateral_flexion.name: Anti Lateral Flexion — Playbook
playbook_anti_lateral_flexion.execution:
Anti-lateral flexion is the least-trained core pattern in commercial fitness and the most valuable for IMS clients. The pattern is essential anytime a client is carrying or lifting unilateral load - from groceries to a suitcase carry to a single-arm overhead press. We train it with progression, because the side plank alone won't cut it for most adults.

The focus is on the quadratus lumborum and hip abductors (the lateral sling) working with the obliques. When this sling is strong, clients protect their spine under asymmetric load. When it's weak, they lean, compensate, and eventually develop unilateral hip or low back pain. This is one of the most overlooked patterns in aging populations - and one of the most valuable.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Hips stacked, ribs stacked.' Straight line head to heel in every side plank variation. No lean, no hike.
WATCH FOR: Hip hike (dominant side up). Usually top hip. Reset or regress.
WATCH FOR: Hip sag (dominant side down). Lateral sling failing. Stop and reset.
WATCH FOR: Shoulder collapse in side plank. Bottom shoulder shrugs or the scap caves. Cue 'shoulder away from ear, elbow stacked under shoulder.'
WATCH FOR: Neck shortening. Crown of head long, shoulder away from ear.
WATCH FOR: Breath-holding - same rule as every brace exercise, breath flows through the hold.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to knee-supported side plank. Reduce lever, rebuild.
PAIR WITH: Glute med activation (side-lying, clam shells), hip CARs with abduction emphasis.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Anti-lateral flexion gets equal attention to anti-extension and anti-rotation. All three plane-resisting core demands trained.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Unilateral carries do double duty - they're carries AND anti-lateral flexion work.

playbook_dynamic_warmup.name: Dynamic Warmup — Playbook
playbook_dynamic_warmup.execution:
The dynamic warm-up at IMS is not a workout. It's preparation - specifically, raising core temperature, activating the nervous system, and moving through ranges of motion the client will use in the session. A good warm-up is 5-8 minutes, leaves the client breathing slightly heavier and moving better, and never fatigues them before the real work.

We do NOT program the warm-up as filler. Every movement earns its place. We cycle warm-up components based on the day's work - a hinge day opens with hinge-preparatory movement; a press day opens with thoracic and shoulder prep; a squat day opens with hip and ankle work. Clients who skip the warm-up pay for it - so the warm-up is built into every session, every block, every program.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Move with intent. You're preparing, not performing.' Light, controlled, purposeful.
WATCH FOR: Client treating the warm-up as a workout. Too fast, too hard, too long. Remind them this is prep.
WATCH FOR: Client skipping rounds because they feel fine. The goal is to feel PREPARED, not fine. Prepared = ready to perform.
WATCH FOR: Pain or restriction during warm-up - this is diagnostic information for the session. Adjust the session based on what you see.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: If a movement causes pain, substitute with a similar-intent alternative. Never just skip.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: 5-8 minutes total. Longer is overkill. Shorter is insufficient.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Warm-up is not negotiable. A client who wants to skip it doesn't get to skip it. Non-negotiable part of the session.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Cycle warm-up components - same warm-up every session creates adaptation, and clients stop paying attention.

playbook_joint_integrity.name: Joint Integrity — Playbook
playbook_joint_integrity.execution:
Joint integrity and corrective work at IMS is how we address the specific movement faults identified during assessment. Unlike the CARs library (which is articular capacity training) or PAILs/RAILs (which is capsular adaptation), the corrective category covers the intermediate ground - activation work, positional re-education, and simple movements that remind the body how to do the thing that isn't happening naturally.

This category is programmed two ways: (1) in warm-ups for activation and movement prep, and (2) as 'fillers' between strength sets - 30-60 seconds of targeted corrective work during rest. We don't program correctives as main blocks for most clients - they're integrated into the session rather than isolated. The exception is new or post-injury clients, who may spend the first 2-4 weeks almost entirely in correctives until basic patterns are cleaned up.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Slow. Slower than you think.' Correctives done fast don't correct anything.
WATCH FOR: Client doing correctives as reps to get through. If it's sloppy, it's not corrective - it's just movement.
WATCH FOR: Compensation patterns during correctives. If bird dog is full of trunk rotation, the correction isn't working. Regress or reset.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Simpler version of the same pattern, or a different corrective targeting the same limitation.
PAIR WITH: Main strength work as inter-set fillers - 30-60 seconds during rest. Corrective + strength in one session.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Integrate, don't isolate. Correctives serve the main lifts, not the other way around.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Select based on assessment. Random correctives = no correction. Hip restriction = hip correctives. Shoulder issue = shoulder correctives.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: New clients and post-injury clients may get 2-4 weeks of corrective-heavy work before loaded training begins.

playbook_decel_accel.name: Decel Accel — Playbook
playbook_decel_accel.execution:
Most IMS clients are not competitive athletes - they're adults who want to stay athletic. The decel/accel category exists for those clients who still participate in sport (pickleball, tennis, basketball, golf, weekend soccer) and for training that transfers to real life - avoiding falls, changing direction safely, reacting to environmental demands.

We program this category sparingly and specifically. For most 35-65 clients, the goal is to maintain reactive ability, not build elite speed. The real value is deceleration training - the ability to stop and reabsorb force - because that's what aging adults lose first. A client who can decelerate well has better balance, falls less, and returns to sport with less injury risk.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Control is faster than speed.' A client who can't control the movement won't sprint safely.
WATCH FOR: Collapsed knees during decel. Drive the knees out, not in.
WATCH FOR: Chest popping up on start. Client rises too fast, loses forward drive.
WATCH FOR: Over-striding. Steps too long for the speed - typically with decel.
WATCH FOR: Arms not matching legs. Opposite arm/leg coordination.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to static positioning or jump-and-stick. Drill the shape, drill the landing.
PAIR WITH: Lower body strength work - squats, RFESS, deadlifts. Speed comes from strength.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Low volume, high quality. 10-20 minutes max per session. Fatigue kills sprint mechanics.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Only for clients who need it. A 60-year-old with no sport demands doesn't need sprint training - gait work suffices.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Start with decel before accel. Absorb before produce.

playbook_power_plyo.name: Power Plyo — Playbook
playbook_power_plyo.execution:
Power output declines faster than strength with age. A 60-year-old who can still deadlift 200 lbs but can't jump off one foot has lost something specific and important - rate of force development. IMS programs power work for every client who's medically cleared for it, because the ability to produce force quickly is life-protective: preventing falls, catching balance, generating speed.

We program power conservatively. Low volume (10-20 reps per session), long rest (1-2 minutes between sets), high quality (every rep is fast and clean, not grinded through). Jump and stick variations cover 80% of what adult clients need. Loaded power work (landmine push press, heavy KB swings) is added for clients with a strength foundation and specific power goals.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Fast on the way up, soft on the way down.' Explode up, absorb on landing.
WATCH FOR: Knees caving at landing. Collapse pattern - stop the set.
WATCH FOR: Slow bar/body speed. Power training is only power training if it's fast. If it's slow, it's strength training with bad form.
WATCH FOR: Chest popping up in jump. Forward lean lost - compromises force direction.
WATCH FOR: Loud landings. Good landings are quiet. Loud = absorbing with bones, not muscles.
WATCH FOR: Breath holding on KB swing. Exhale at the top, inhale on the way down.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Back to jump and stick (lower height or box). Rebuild landing quality.
PAIR WITH: Ankle and hip CARs, lower body strength work. Power is force + speed - you need both.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Low volume. 10-20 reps per session total. Rest is long (1-2 minutes between sets).
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Power work comes EARLY in the session, when the nervous system is fresh. Not at the end as a finisher.

playbook_conditioning.name: Conditioning — Playbook
playbook_conditioning.execution:
Conditioning at IMS is programmed with specific intent, not as a generic 'get sweaty' dump. We ask what energy system we're training and whether the conditioning modality interferes with other training goals. For most IMS clients, conditioning is moderate intensity, joint-friendly, and volume-controlled - we don't program CrossFit-style 'workout of the day' sessions because the cost-benefit doesn't serve our population.

Our default conditioning modalities favor low-impact, high-recovery tools: sled work, carries, airbike, rower, and KB complexes. These build aerobic and mixed-energy-system capacity without trashing joints, interfering with strength adaptation, or requiring long recovery windows. We avoid high-impact running, complex barbell metcons, and AMRAP-style density work for most adult clients - not because they don't work, but because they don't fit the IMS population's goals.
coach_cues: THE CUE THAT ALWAYS APPLIES: 'Match your effort to the intent.' Conditioning isn't 'go until you can't.' It's 'work to the target intensity, recover, repeat.'
WATCH FOR: Form breakdown under conditioning load. Stop before form fails.
WATCH FOR: Breath-holding during KB swings or complexes. Breath must flow.
WATCH FOR: Client ego-pushing intervals - going harder than programmed because 'it felt easy.' Defeats the point.
WATCH FOR: Carry-over fatigue into strength sessions. If conditioning is compromising strength days, reduce volume or change timing.
DEFAULT REGRESSION: Lower intensity modality or longer rest intervals. Simpler, safer conditioning always available.
PAIR WITH: Recovery modalities at IMS (sauna, Normatec, massage) to manage fatigue. Conditioning without recovery is just digging a hole.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Purposeful, not punitive. Every conditioning session has a specific energy system target.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: 2x per week is enough for most IMS clients. More than that often interferes with strength.
PROGRAMMING PRINCIPLE: Don't program conditioning on the same day as heavy strength work unless the client is a specific athlete who needs it.
